  :                            9 .    RTA1 is a free processor architecture specification.

  :                            11 .    The executable emulation of RTA1 is free software.
  :                            12 .
  :                            13 .    This instruction code for the target RTA1 architecture is free software
  :                            14 .
  :                            15 .    Software programs delivered with this software to connect the
  :                            16 .    emulated RTA1 with real network interfaces in the emulator host
  :                            17 .    are free software
  :                            18 .
  :                            19 .    Scripts and programs delivered with this software for running
  :                            20 .    on other computers and interacting with the RTA1 are free software
  :                            21 .
  :                            22 .    The masmx target-independent meta-asembler delivered here
  :                            23 .    is free software whether it is used for constructing RTA1 code
  :                            24 .    or any other code. masmx is a separate free program by the
  :                            25 .    same author and included with this software to save searching for it

  :                            53 .	measure is once-only initialisation code
  :                            54 .	called at kernel start up
  :                            55 .	it is in a lcal subroutine to make it

  :                            57 .	any other bank management routines
  :                            58 .	in this file

00:00108D A80458              +116 	andB	(03fffff)	. isolate the highest page index
00:00108E CD0001              +117 	ab, xi	1		. convert the highest index (+1)
  :                            118 				. to the number of pages
  :                            119 
00:00108F 600085              +120 	la	b_x
00:001090 A5FFC0              +121 	and, xi	-64
00:001091 060005              +122 	sar	5		. calculate and save
00:001092 200086              +123 	sa	BANKS		. the # words potentially needed
  :                            124 				. for descriptors of 256K banks
  :                            125 
  :                            126 				. the big banks descriptor stack
  :                            127 				. has space for a potential two-word
  :                            128 				. descriptor for each 262144-word bank
  :                            129 				. actually present in system memory
  :                            130 
  :                            131 				. the descriptor values used for
  :                            132 				. system memory in IO-mapped
  :                            133 				. relocation registers have 6
  :                            134 				. low zero bits when
  :                            135 				. descriptor bit 22 (BIG/BANK)
  :                            136 				. is on
  :                            137 
  :                            138 				. 01xxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx 000000
  :                            139 
  :                            140 				. because when BIG/BANK is on,
  :                            141 				. LS 6 bits in the relocation
  :                            142 				. register port select devices other
  :                            143 				. than system memory (filestore arrays,
  :                            144 				. dual ported network I/F memories
  :                            145 
  :                            146 				. 01xxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx DEVICE
  :                            147 
  :                            148 				. however, when BIG/BANK is off,
  :                            149 				. the six low bits select a page
  :                            150 				. within the system memory bank
  :                            151 
  :                            152 				. 00xxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx XXXXXX
  :                            153 
  :                            154 
  :                            155 
  :                            156 				. construct the first descriptor
  :                            157 				. which describes the end of memory
  :                            158 				. at the end of OS tabler memory
  :                            159

  :                            249 				. *next points to next_descriptor
  :                            250 				. for small banks
  :                            251 				. this is the next available two-word
  :                            252 				. descriptor in the upward-growing
  :                            253 				. small banks decriptor list
  :                            254 
  :                            255 				. *next and the initial descriptor
  :                            256 				. where *next points are constructed
  :                            257 				. here at system start, so all
  :                            258 				. system images are restartable
  :                            259 				. and may be in ROM
  :                            260 
  :                            261 				. The buffer pointer in the initial
  :                            262 				. *next descriptor  points to the
  :                            263 				. first available RAM page after
  :                            264 				. the kernel ROM or kernel load
  :                            265 
  :                            266 				. there are 64 free-chains into the
  :                            267 				. "small" banks heap and one free-chain 
  :                            268 				. into the big-banks stack
  :                            269 	
  :                            270 				. The small-banks free-chains are one
  :                            271 				. per size, 1..64 pages (4096 words
  :                            272 				. to 262144 words)
  :                            273 
  :                            274 				. a "small" bank of 262144 words is
  :                            275 				. possible, because a smaller bank
  :                            276 				. may get bigger if the next assignment
  :                            277 				. does not fit in the same 262144-word
  :                            278 				. physical bank
  :                            279 
  :                            280 				. small banks are assigned from low-order
  :                            281 				. locations upwards. The free chains
  :                            282 				. reassign free storage at the lowest
  :                            283 				. addresses first.
  :                            284 
  :                            285 				. Freed space above the highest
  :                            286 				. assigned small bank is recombined
  :                            287 				. as single free area between the
  :                            288 				. the stack of small banks which grows
  :                            289 				. upwards and compresses downwards, and
  :                            290 				. the stack of big banks, which grows
  :                            291 				. downwards and compresses upwards
  :                            292 
  :                            293 				. There is one large-banks free-chain
  :                            294 				. for all sizes. A big bank may combine
  :                            295 				. one to nearly 65536 adjacent physical
  :                            296 				. banks of 262144 words
  :                            297 
  :                            298 				. A single large bank may contain nearly
  :                            299 				. 1024 times the 16 Megaword Address space
  :                            300 				.          -16 gigawords in total-
  :                            301 				. for large arrays which need to be contiguous
  :                            302 
  :                            303 				. An array needs to be contiguous if
  :                            304 				. multiword operands to single instructions
  :                            305 				. straddle physical 256K bank boundaries.
  :                            306 
  :                            307 				. Developers can also structure arrays
  :                            308 				. so that quad or double word operands
  :                            309 				. are safely inside one bank, even if
  :                            310 				. the containing structure straddles two
  :                            311 				. non-contiguous banks addressed with
  :                            312 				. two adjacent relocation base-registers
  :                            313 
  :                            314 				. Operand EA is translated to an absolute
  :                            315 				. address once for each operand read or write
  :                            316 
  :                            317 				. However very large contiguous bank
  :                            318 				. arrays are obtained with one ARRAY$ call
  :                            319 				. and accessed with one bank handle
  :                            320 
  :                            321 				. Arrays larger than the address space
  :                            322 				. are addressed a part at
  :                            323 				. a time. Structures straddling bank boundaries
  :                            324 				. are accessed using two adjacent base registers
  :                            325 				. to address two adjacent banks
  :                            326 
  :                            327 	.
  :                            328 	.	lb	instance_index		. read the subscript
  :                            329 	.	m, xi	STRUCTURE_SIZE		. convert to table offset
  :                            330 	.	dsl	6			. split into relative
  :                            331 	.	sal	6			. bank/word offsets

  :                            334 	.					. large_bank_request routine

  :                            339 
  :                            340 	.	sabr	BASE_REGISTER_TAG	. base the bank where the 
  :                            341 	.					. structure starts
  :                            342 
  :                            343 	.	la, i	64, a			. and base the next bank after
  :                            344 	.	sabr	BASE_REGISTER_TAG+1	. in the next range of the
  :                            345 	.					. address space
  :                            346 
  :                            347 				. where for addresses > 262143, the
  :                            348 				. storage operand address is resolved
  :                            349 				. with the base register indicated in
  :                            350 				. effective address bits 23..18
  :                            351 
  :                            352 				.	_____________________________
  :                            353 				.	|baseid|xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx|
  :                            354 				.	|______|____________________|
  :                            355 				.
  :                            356 				. and for addresses 256..262143, the 
  :                            357 				. storage operand address is resolved
  :                            358 				. with the base register indicated in
  :                            359 				. effective address bits 17..12
  :                            360 				.
  :                            361 				.	_____________________________
  :                            362 				.	|000000|baseid|xxxxxx xxxxxx|
  :                            363 				.	|______|______|_____________|
  :                            364 				.
  :                            365 				. instructions are always fetched from
  :                            366 				. the bank based with base register zero
  :                            367 				. whatever the effective address of the
  :                            368 				. instruction
  :                            369 				.
  :                            370 				. storage operands in the
  :                            371 				. address range 256..4095 are also in
  :                            372 				. instruction bank 0.
  :                            373 
  :                            374 				. storage operands at effective address <
  :                            375 				. 256 are registers.
  :                            376 
  :                            377 				. large banks are assigned from high-order
  :                            378 				. memory downwards, in size multiples of
  :                            379 				. one bank (262144 words) The highest-addressed
  :                            380 				. contiguous area large enough for each
  :                            381 				. request is assigned. This is achieved
  :                            382 				. by recombining and reshaping the
  :                            383 				. available contiguous collections of
  :                            384 				. 262144-word banks
  :                            385 
  :                            386 				. "small" banks may accidentally become
  :                            387 				. an aligned bank of 64 pages, if the next
  :                            388 				. assignment steps to the next bank edge
  :                            389 				. ("small" banks don't straddle physical
  :                            390 				. bank edges). Because of this, a task

  :                            392 				. have it

  :                            5 .    This file is instruction code for the freeware processor architecture
  :                            6 .
  :                            7 .                RTA1
  :                            8 .
  :                            9 .    RTA1 is a free processor architecture specification.
  :                            10 .
  :                            11 .    The executable emulation of RTA1 is free software.
  :                            12 .
  :                            13 .    This instruction code for the target RTA1 architecture is free software
  :                            14 .
  :                            15 .    Software programs delivered with this software to connect the
  :                            16 .    emulated RTA1 with real network interfaces in the emulator host
  :                            17 .    are free software
  :                            18 .
  :                            19 .    Scripts and programs delivered with this software for running
  :                            20 .    on other computers and interacting with the RTA1 are free software
  :                            21 .
  :                            22 .    The masmx target-independent meta-asembler delivered here
  :                            23 .    is free software whether it is used for constructing RTA1 code
  :                            24 .    or any other code. masmx is a separate free program by the
  :                            25 .    same author and included with this software to save searching for it
  :                            26 .
  :                            27 .    Scripts and utility programs for constructing RTA1 target
  :                            28 .    executable software are free software

  :                            32 .    the Free Software Foundation, either version 3 of the License, or

  :                            286 
  :                            287 .	each handle integer corresponds to a 48-bit encoding
  :                            288 .	starting at handle(1)
  :                            289 
  :                            290 .	the index corresponds to a port number or to a
  :                            291 .	TCP connection and is the encoded offset of the
  :                            292 .	control block in the containing bank
  :                            293 
  :                            294 .	47	40 39		     24	 23				0
  :                            295 .	_________________________________________________________________
  :                            296 .	|hndl type|	 index 16 bits	| descriptor of containing bank	|
  :                            297 .	|_________|_____________________|_______________________________|

  :                            400 
  :                            401 
  :                            402 .	these are here to read or write
  :                            403 .	a pointer in the kernel vector bank
  :                            404 
  :                            405 .	from routines which might be called
  :                            406 .	from either a kernel task or an
  :                            407 .	application
  :                            408 
  :                            409 .	see$2 makes the kernel vector bank
  :                            410 .	visible instead of the application
  :                            411 .	vector bank
  :                            412 
  :                            413 .	on return from ISR, see$2 is on or off
  :                            414 .	whichever it was before

  :                            216 .	    A	    B	    6	    7
  :                            217 .	_________________________________
  :                            218 .	| socket|   0	|address:port	|
  :                            219 .	|_______|_______|_______________|
  :                            220 
  :                            221 .	note the kernel vector bank is read and
  :                            222 .	written with internal interrupts
  :                            223 
  :                            224 .		ii	READ_XVECTOR
  :                            225 .		ii	WRITE_XVECTOR
  :                            226 
  :                            227 .	because this routine is available
  :                            228 .	both to kernel tasks which share
  :                            229 .	the kernel vector bank, and unkernel
  :                            230 .	tasks which have their own vector
  :                            231 .	bank
  :                            232 
  :                            233 .	the internal ISRs switch to the
  :                            234 .	kernel vector bank
  :                            235 
  :                            236 .		on	see$2
  :                            237 
  :                            238 .	return from any ISR restores all PSR
  :                            239 .	flags to what they were on II
  :                            240 
  :                            241 .	the kernel task which mostly calls here
  :                            242 .	is the IP task in this bank,
  :                            243 .	because some application server protocols
  :                            244 .	are in this task
  :                            245 
  :                            246 .	otherwise the callers are application
  :                            247 .	tasks

  :                            67: 	$include	i_f
  :                            1 .       
  :                            2 .    Copyright Tim Cox, 2012
  :                            3 .    TimMilesCox@gmx.ch
  :                            4 .
  :                            5 .    This file is instruction code for the freeware processor architecture
  :                            6 .
  :                            7 .                RTA1
  :                            8 .
  :                            9 .    RTA1 is a free processor architecture specification.
  :                            10 .
  :                            11 .    The executable emulation of RTA1 is free software.
  :                            12 .
  :                            13 .    This instruction code for the target RTA1 architecture is free software
  :                            14 .
  :                            15 .    Software programs delivered with this software to connect the
  :                            16 .    emulated RTA1 with real network interfaces in the emulator host
  :                            17 .    are free software
  :                            18 .
  :                            19 .    Scripts and programs delivered with this software for running
  :                            20 .    on other computers and interacting with the RTA1 are free software
  :                            21 .
  :                            22 .    The masmx target-independent meta-asembler delivered here
  :                            23 .    is free software whether it is used for constructing RTA1 code
  :                            24 .    or any other code. masmx is a separate free program by the
  :                            25 .    same author and included with this software to save searching for it
  :                            26 .
  :                            27 .    Scripts and utility programs for constructing RTA1 target
  :                            28 .    executable software are free software      
  :                            29 .
  :                            30 .    You can redistribute it and/or modify RTA1
  :                            31 .    under the terms of the GNU General Public License as published by
  :                            32 .    the Free Software Foundation, either version 3 of the License, or
  :                            33 .    (at your option) any later version.
  :                            34 .
  :                            35 .    RTA1 is distributed in the hope that it will be useful,
  :                            36 .    but WITHOUT ANY WARRANTY; without even the implied warranty of
  :                            37 .    MERCHANTABILITY or FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE.  See the
  :                            38 .    GNU General Public License for more details.
  :                            39 .

  :                            41 .    along with RTA1.  If not, see <http://www.gnu.org/licenses/>.
  :                            42 .
  :                            43 .
  :                            44 
  :                            45 . Emulation implements one kind of network interface
  :                            46 
  :                            47 . this can be modified when there is more than one interface type
  :                            48 . initially the only known type is modelled as a dual ported memory
  :                            49 . interface to the system
  :                            50 .
  :                            51 . half of the interfacing memory presents input frames
  :                            52 . the other half is available for output frames
  :                            53 
  :                            54 
  :                            55 .		_________________________________
  :                            56 .	>------>|1st receive buffer		|
  :                            57 .	|	|2nd				|
  :                            58 .	|	|				|
  :                            59 .	^
  :                            60 .	|
  :                            61 .	<-------|last receive buffer		|
  :                            62 .		|_______________________________|
  :                            63 .	>------>|1st transmit buffer		|
  :                            64 .	|	|2nd				|
  :                            65 .	|	|				|
  :                            66 .	^
  :                            67 .	|
  :                            68 .	<-------|last transmit buffer		|
  :                            69 .		|_______________________________|
  :                            70 
  :                            71 
  :                            72 . the frames arrive and are sent in cyclic order of the buffers
  :                            73 .
  :                            74 . there is an 8-word length + ethernet header at the start of each buffer
  :                            75 . this sketch represents a quartet of bits per symbol
  :                            76 
  :                            77 .	 00LLLL 00xxxx 00xxxx 00xxxx 00yyyy 00yyyy 00yyyy 00pppp
  :                            78 
  :                            79 .	________________________________________________________________________
  :                            80 .	| 00LLLL | 00xxxx | 00xxxx | 00xxxx | 00yyyy | 00yyyy | 00yyyy | 00pppp|
  :                            81 .	|________|________|________|________|________|________|________|_______|
  :                            82 .	| 00data | 00....
  :                            83 
  :                            84 
  :                            85 . network data is buffered 16 bits per computer word right up to the socket
  :                            86 . and XDR interfaces. These "interface dual ported" memories may be 16-bit
  :                            87 . memories reading out eight zero bits at the high order positions of each
  :                            88 . computer word, or they may be 24-bit memories: then the high-order 8 bits
  :                            89 . must be maintained with zero values
  :                            90 
  :                            91 .	LLLL	is frame size, and nonzero value written here indicates
  :                            92 .		a complete frame received, or available for transmission
  :                            93 .
  :                            94 .	xxxx xxxx xxxx	is the destination ethernet address
  :                            95 
  :                            96 .	yyyy yyyy yyyy	is the source ethernet address
  :                            97 
  :                            98 .	pppp	is the protocol family indicator, 0800 for IPv4, 0806 for ARP
  :                            99 .		as many as required
  :                            100 
  :                            101 
  :                            102 . the buffers are equal sized and used cyclically
  :                            103 
  :                            104 . the input machine signals to software that a frame is available
  :                            105 . by writing a length in bits 15:0 of the first word in the buffer:
  :                            106 .
  :                            107 .		0000 0000 LLLL LLLL LLLL LLLL
  :                            108 .
  :                            109 . The length is not self-inclusive. It is the frame length.
  :                            110 . The frame starts at the next word
  :                            111 
  :                            112 . software indicates that the frame has been sampled and the buffer is
  :                            113 . free by writing zero to this length word
  :                            114 
  :                            115 . software indicates that a frame for transmission is assembled 
  :                            116 . in the transmission buffer by writing a length in bits 15:0 of
  :                            117 . the first word in the transmission buffer. The length is the
  :                            118 . frame length and not self-inclusive
  :                            119 
  :                            120 . The frame for transmission starts in the second word of the
  :                            121 . transmission buffer
  :                            122 
  :                            123 . Transmission completion is indicated back to the software by zero
  :                            124 . in the length word, and this also indicates the transmission buffer
  :                            125 . is again free
  :                            126 
  :                            127 . This interface model is not part of RTA architecture but supports
  :                            128 . this emulation and is intended to work like network interfaces are 
  :                            129 . intended for RTA systems
  :                            130 . 
  :                            131 . That is, some of the 63 DEVICEs (arrays outside system memory)
  :                            132 . should carry network data to and from an RTA system.
  :                            133 
  :                            134 . A DEVICE is up to 65536 BANKS of up to 262144 WORDS of up to 24 bits
  :                            135 .
  :                            136 
  :                            137 . device 0 is system memory or executable space and is up to 16 gigawords
  :                            138 . of 24-bit memory
  :                            139 
  :                            140 . only system memory is selectable in pages of 4096 words as well as in
  :                            141 . banks of 256K
  :                            142 
  :                            143 . Memory in the other 63 possible devices is selectable in banks of
  :                            144 . 262144 words only
  :                            145 
  :                            146 . the pointer to a page of system memory based in one of the 72 relocation
  :                            147 . registers has the layout
  :                            148 
  :                            149 .	00pp pppp pppp pppp pppp pppp
  :                            150 
  :                            151 . where bit 22 = 0 indicates a 22-bit page pointer and the task has a granule
  :                            152 . of 4096 words addressed and available
  :                            153 
  :                            154 . the pointer to a bank of system memory in a relocation register has the layout
  :                            155 
  :                            156 .	01pp pppp pppp pppp pp00 0000
  :                            157 
  :                            158 . where bit 22 = 1 indicates a 16-bit bank pointer and the task has a granule
  :                            159 . of 262144 words addressed and available
  :                            160 
  :                            161 . bits 5:0 = 00 0000 indicate that the bank is in system memory
  :                            162 .
  :                            163 . the pointer to a bank of "I/O Memory" (strictly an array other than
  :                            164 . system memory) has the layout in a relocation register
  :                            165 
  :                            166 .	01bb bbbb bbbb bbbb bbDE VICE
  :                            167 
  :                            168 . where bit 22 = 1 = Big Bank ** DEVICE = nonzero = some other device than
  :                            169 . system memory
  :                            170 
  :                            171 . Some of these 63 possible arrays outside system memory will be battery-
  :                            172 . supported SRAMS of 24-bit words containing realtime transaction file systems
  :                            173 
  :                            174 . Others may be flash memories containing default system images and occasional
  :                            175 . data backups (but flash memories may not be used for high-frequency
  :                            176 . transaction update data)
  :                            177 
  :                            178 . some may be insertable flashes containing 16- or 8-bit memories organised
  :                            179 . in a file system architecture readable and writable by other systems
  :                            180 
  :                            181 . some may be video RAMs of up to 16 Gigawords of 24-bit words
  :                            182 
  :                            183 . At least one device must be an array interfacing a network adaptor to the
  :                            184 . RTA system with 16 network data bits per computer word as modelled here
  :                            185 
  :                            186 . This interface array is supplied to the emulator by a Unix shared memory
  :                            187 . block of 524288 bytes which looks to RTA like a device of one bank of
  :                            188 . 262144 16-bit words.
  :                            189 
  :                            190 . RTA reads each word out as a 24-bit computer word with bits 23:16 = 0000 0000
  :                            191 . The first half of this block is receive buffers and the second half transmit
  :                            192 . buffers
  :                            193 
  :                            194 . the settings here are that RPAGEW is 1024 words. This is the buffer size
  :                            195 . and it allows an ethernet frame of 1514+ bytes. Jumbos are not accounted
  :                            196 . for in the emulation. A device in a large-configuration RTA may have
  :                            197 . 65536 * 262144 words in a network interface device, but the emulator has
  :                            198 . only 1 * 262144 words in its one network interface space.
  :                            199 
  :                            200 . Therefore the emulator will only test standard ethernet traffic
  :                            201 
  :                            202 . the configuration of the emulated interface (which links to a real interface
  :                            203 . outside the emulator) is therefore
  :                            204 
  :                            205 .	128 receive buffers of 1024 words each
  :                            206 . plus	128 transmit buffers of 1024 words each
  :                            207 
  :                            208 
  :                            209 . Notes. Note 1
  :                            210 
  :                            211 . To avoid accidental damage, device banks (as distinct from system memory
  :                            212 . banks) may only be based by interrupt code. This avoids inadvertant
  :                            213 . traversal of array accesses across all the devices. At its simplest,
  :                            214 . the internal interrupts (system calls II 64+32 .. II 64+63) only need
  :                            215 . to copy the bank handle from user-A register, base it, and return:
  :                            216 
  :                            217 .		la	A
  :                            218 .		sabr	32
  :                            219 .		ir	0
  :                            220 
  :                            221 		.
  :                            222 		.
  :                            223 
  :                            224 .		la	A
  :                            225 .		sabr	63
  :                            226 .		ir	0
  :                            227 
  :                            228 . but as much extra security can be added to that as wished in more complex OS
  :                            229 
  :                            230 . Once the storage area is based, the application can access it at will.
  :                            231 . If this is not wished, the interrupt code itself must carry out required
  :                            232 . operations and unbase the storage area before return. The simplest model
  :                            233 . here relies on trusted applications which base the sensitive banks
  :                            234 . by design and not by accident
  :                            235 
  :                            236 . This also provides the beginning of a security model for any implementation
  :                            237 . which includes the translatable storage handle
  :                            238 
  :                            239 . Where a handle 0xxx xxxx xxxx xxxx xxxx is absolute whether it points
  :                            240 . to system memory or a device
  :                            241 
  :                            242 . 1xxx xxxx xxxx xxxx xxxx is reserved for a possible translating range of
  :                            243 . storage area handles, for moveable/swappable application storage.
  :                            244 . A hardware implementation which does translatable handles would reserve
  :                            245 . the basing of all absolute areas (device and system memory) to privileged
  :                            246 . code of some sort, perhaps system calls as in this initial model
  :                            247 
  :                            248 . applications could then only directly base an allowed set of areas described
  :                            249 . in their translate lists.
  :                            250 
  :                            251 
  :                            252 
  :                            253 . Note 2
  :                            254 
  :                            255 . the interface handler here will offer each frame to the protocol family
  :                            256 . (mostly IPv4 or similar), which must decide immediately whether to take
  :                            257 . the frame and place it in storage visible to the application.
  :                            258 
  :                            259 . This means IP will check IP correctness, and call ICMP, UDP or TCP which
  :                            260 . must checksum as necessary while copying the datagram to socket storage.
  :                            261 
  :                            262 . This is not a flat-address system and there is no zero-copy
  :                            263 . receive. But there is only one copy, and it is carried out during checksum
  :                            264 . generation. After that the datagrams are in socket-associated buffers which
  :                            265 . are placed in the address space of the application.
  :                            266 
  :                            267 . The socket or XDR interface will format the data when the application
  :                            268 . subsequently receives it over the socket API.
  :                            269 
  :                            270 . on return from the call to the protocol family, the interface buffer is
  :                            271 . freed
  :                            272 
  :                            273 
  :                            274 
  :                            275 RX_LL_HL $equ	d$save
  :                            276 
  :                            277 
  :                            278 
  :                            279 $(1)
  :                            280 
  :                            281 .	pointer array to the network interface descriptors
  :                            282 .	these are numbered starting from one as in MIB-II
  :                            283 .	interfaces table
  :                            284 
  :                            285 .	word zero of this table is a count of the number of
  :                            286 .	interface descriptors in use
  :                            287 
  :                            288 .	the initial value 1 indicates that there is one
  :                            289 .	interface so far, interface(1) = internal loopback,
  :                            290 .	and the next to be configured ought to be interface(2)
  :                            291 
  :                            292 .	to obtain a descriptor for a new interface, increment
  :                            293 .	the counter in the first word of the array and select
  :                            294 .	the pointer at
  :                            295 
  :                            296 .	i_f_pointer_array[i_f_pointer_array[0]]
  :                            297 
  :                            298 .	for example
  :                            299 .
  :                            300 .		lx		i_f_pointer_array
  :                            301 .		ax, xi		1
  :                            302 .		sx		i_f_pointer_array
  :                            303 .		lx		0, x
  :                            304 
  :                            305 
  :                            306 i_f_pointer_array	. who wants to go multiplying subscripts?

  :                            156 
  :                            157 nowhere_to_go_netbank
  :                            158 
  :                            159 .	if we couldn't get 8K for network buffers
  :                            160 .	then we won't start any new tasks either
  :                            161 
  :                            162 .	we should maybe set an LED pattern
  :                            163 .	and exit the IP process
  :                            164 
  :                            165 .	otherwise
  :                            166 .	we could program a flash to say it's always full
  :                            167 .	and no buffer is ever available in it
  :                            168

  :                            180 net_transfer
  :                            181 .	from q is y
  :                            182 .	to q is a
  :                            183 .	strictly from head of fromq
  :                            184 .	to tail of 2q
  :                            185 
  :                            186 	push	a
  :                            187 
  :                            188 net_transfer1
  :                            189 	lcal	readq
  :                            190 	jza	net_transferx

  :                            16 .    emulated RTA1 with real network interfaces in the emulator host
  :                            17 .    are free software

  :                            20 .    on other computers and interacting with the RTA1 are free software

  :                            23 .    is free software whether it is used for constructing RTA1 code
  :                            24 .    or any other code. masmx is a separate free program by the
  :                            25 .    same author and included with this software to save searching for it

  :                            45 HEADER_WORDS	$equ	d$save	. 16 used in receive
  :                            46 HEADER_LONGWORDS $equ	d$save1	. 17 used in receive
  :                            47 TP_HEADER	$equ	d$save2	. 18 used in receive and transmit
  :                            48 I_F_IDX		$equ	d$save3	. 19 used in receive
  :                            49 
  :                            50 TX_AXW		$equ	8	. used in transmit
  :                            51 TX_FRAME	$equ	9	. used in receive and transmit
  :                            52 
  :                            53 HEADER_BYTES	$equ	d$save	. 16 used in transmit
  :                            54 MTU		$equ	d$save1	. 17 used in transmit
  :                            55 I_F		$equ	d$save2	. 19 used in transmit
  :                            56 
  :                            57 TX_FLAG_OFFSET	$equ	10	. used in tranmsit
  :                            58 TX_DATAGRAML	$equ	11	. used in transmit = unfragmented datagram
  :                            59 				. generated in interfaces = xmitted frame
  :                            60 
  :                            61 DYNAMIC_IP_HEADER $equ	0
  :                            62 		. if IP header length cannot be known at socket layer
  :                            63 		. then make this a multiple of 4 > 20 and < 64
  :                            64 		. to give a fixed large space for IP to add header
  :                            65 		. options
  :                            66 
  :                            67 		. otherwise IP header size is fixed when the socket
  :                            68 		. layer acquires a buffer
  :                            69

  :                            307 
  :                            308 .	ipv4_rx, input driver for a system with fixed dual ported
  :                            309 .	interface buffers
  :                            310 
  :                            311 .	this is an upward call from routine frames
  :                            312 
  :                            313 .	copying to socket-visible buffers is combined with
  :                            314 .	checksumming
  :                            315 
  :                            316 .	each word in the interface buffers is read only once
  :                            317 
  :                            318 .	ipv4_rx pushes and checks the IP headers in the
  :                            319 .	internal stack, which is a register array
  :                            320 
  :                            321 .	upper protocols obtain a buffer, then pop the IP headers
  :                            322 .	into it ahead of their own protocol message for
  :                            323 .	checksumming during transfer
  :                            324

  :                            61 udp_next

  :                            90 udp_nosocket

  :                            96 udp4_x
00:00239F 350000              +97 	lret	0
  :                            98 
  :                            99 
  :                            100 .	this is the direct feed to udpq from interfaces which have
  :                            101 .	fixed dual-ported buffering. Interfaces with a direct access
  :                            102 .	to system memory would not have this packet copy
  :                            103 
  :                            104 .	The packet copy is used as an occasion to checksum
  :                            105 
  :                            106 .	The packet copy places the packets in rx_buffer with
  :                            107 .	T77 descriptors which are called ACWs
  :                            108 
  :                            109 .	This routine cannot be called for fragmented datagrams
  :                            110 .	because it is instant from frame readout to UDP checksum
  :                            111 .	generation
  :                            112 
  :                            113 .	this is an upward call from ipv4_rx
  :                            114 
00:0023A0 580012              +115 udp_rx	ly	TP_HEADER
  :                            116 
00:0023A1 FD0001              +117 	MIB_ICOUNT48	udpInDatagrams:i
(+01)0f
00:0023A2 2F0406              +117 
00:0023A3 5E03A5              +117 
(+01)0f
00:0023A4 170405              +117 
  :                            118 
00:0023A5 8FB000              +119 	qpush	0, y
00:0023A6 8D0004              +120 	ay, xi	4
00:0023A7 60E003              +121 	la	descriptor:bytes
00:0023A8 D0F002              +122 	ana	uugram:udgraml
00:0023A9 5E0281              +123 	jnc	ipv4_drop
  :                            124 
00:0023AA CFF002              +125 	dl	uugram:udgraml	. length:checksum
00:0023AB C5FFF8              +126 	aa, xi	-8
00:0023AC 5E0281              +127 	jnc	ipv4_drop
00:0023AD 20E003              +128 	sa	descriptor:bytes
00:0023AE DE03B7              +129 	jnzb	udp_rx_check
  :                            130 
00:0023AF 87A000              +131 	qpop	0, x
00:0023B0 850004              +132 	ax, xi	4
00:0023B1 10E006              +133 	sx	descriptor:apdata
  :                            134 
00:0023B2 3E0460              +135 	lcal	rx_nocheck
  :                            136 
(+01)10
00:0023B3 5D02E8              +137 	ly, xi	udpq
00:0023B4 60000E              +138 	la	fp		. the descriptor
00:0023B5 3E0BD4              +139 	lcal	q2
00:0023B6 B60284              +140 	j	ipv4_rxz
  :                            141 
  :                            142 .	this is a protocol which has a length in the header
  :                            143 .	so the first step is to see if the IP datagram is
  :                            144 .	long enough to contain the UDP datagram
  :                            145 .	after that the operative length is the one in
  :                            146 .	the header
  :                            147 
  :                            148 udp_rx_check
00:0023B7 C70010              +149 	ds	LAST_BYTES	. LAST_BYTES:CHECKSUM
  :                            150 
00:0023B8 400004              +151 	lr	a
00:0023B9 CF0006              +152 	dl	6
00:0023BA D7F000              +153 	da	uugram:sourcep
00:0023BB C0F002              +154 	aa	uugram:udgraml
00:0023BC C70006              +155 	ds	6
00:0023BD 600000              +156 	la	r
  :                            157 
00:0023BE 87A000              +158 	qpop	0, x
00:0023BF 850004              +159 	ax, xi	4
00:0023C0 10E006              +160 	sx	descriptor:apdata
  :                            161 
00:0023C1 3E045C              +162 	lcal	udp_rx_payload
  :                            163 
00:0023C2 D00011              +164 	ana	CHECKSUM
00:0023C3 D60281              +165 	jnza	ipv4_drop
  :                            166 
(+01)10
00:0023C4 5D02E8              +167 	ly, xi	udpq
00:0023C5 60000E              +168 	la	fp
00:0023C6 3E0BD4              +169 	lcal	q2
  :                            170 	
00:0023C7 B60284              +171 	j	ipv4_rxz
  :                            172 
  :                            173 
  :                            174 .	this is the feed to udpq for reassembled datagrams
  :                            175 .	which are already in a chain of rx_buffer with ACW
  :                            176 .	descriptors
  :                            177 
  :                            178 .	each fragment has the checksum addition of its data
  :                            179 .	in the ACW, to exploit the chance of checksum
  :                            180 .	generation during copy to rx_buffer
  :                            181 
  :                            182 .	it has been second-guessed in all cases that checksum
  :                            183 .	is specified in the UDP header (the UDP header may not
  :                            184 .	be the first fragment of the datagram to arrive).
  :                            185 
  :                            186 .	also, it is unlikely that UDP payload large enough
  :                            187 .	to need reassembly would be exempt from checksum
  :                            188 
  :                            189 .	no-checksum is usually a feature of high-speed
  :                            190 .	media traffic which is transmitted in much smaller
  :                            191 .	packets than any MTU
  :                            192 
  :                            193 .	large reassembly is more likely to be traffic like
  :                            194 .	NFS in 8KByte slabs (or 3KByte slabs where RTA is the
  :                            195 .	NFS client). Checksum would certainly apply
  :                            196 
  :                            197 .	So all the data arriving here has been end-around
  :                            198 .	added and the positive sum left in the ACW
  :                            199 
  :                            200 .	if the UDP header has a nonzero checksum, the data sums
  :                            201 .	are now summed with network address, protocol index
  :                            202 .	and user datagram length, then inverted and if finally
  :                            203 .	zero inverted again. Then compared.
  :                            204 
  :                            205 	$if	RX_FRAGMENTS
  :                            206 udp_rx_rasm
  :                            207 	lx	descriptor:uproto
  :                            208 	la	uugram:checksum, x
  :                            209 	jza	udp_rx_rasm_nocheck
  :                            210 	push	a
  :                            211 	z	uugram:checksum, x
  :                            212 	lcal	rasm77_gcheck
  :                            213 	tnz	b		. because zero isn't
  :                            214 	xorB,xi	-1		. a UDP checksum
  :                            215 	xorB	0, sp
  :                            216 	pop	uugram:checksum, x
  :                            217 	la	fp
  :                            218 	jnzb	net_free_chain
  :                            219 udp_rx_rasm_nocheck
  :                            220 	la	fp
  :                            221 	lx, xi	udpq	
  :                            222 	j	net_add_chain
  :                            223 	$endif
  :                            224 
  :                            225 
  :                            226 .	call call vector udp_send gets to here
  :                            227 .	other external routines in this bank
  :                            228 .	can do a naughty jump here instead
  :                            229 .	of a far return
  :                            230 
  :                            231 udp_tx

  :                            237 				.		fret	0

  :                            258 udp_txbx
00:0023D7 3D0000              +259 	fret	0
  :                            260 				. this is a far call because
  :                            261 				. socket users may call it
  :                            262 
  :                            263 				. unlike the similar calls
  :                            264 				. in icmp and tcp which are
  :                            265 				. not application visible
  :                            266 
  :                            267 
  :                            268 
  :                            269 . far calls
  :                            270 
  :                            271 .	udp_acw_send
  :                            272 .	udp_acw_send_nocheck
  :                            273 
  :                            274 . get to here
  :                            275 
  :                            276 . udp_txaw
  :                            277 . udp_txaw_nocheck
  :                            278 
  :                            279 . low level call with the acw adress in X
  :                            280 
  :                            281 . both ports must be in the UDP header
  :                            282 . both adresses must be in the address buffer
  :                            283 
  :                            284 . if there is no come-from address in the address buffer
  :                            285 . the thing cannot now be checksummed and an earlier
  :                            286 . call should have done the internal routing step to
  :                            287 . find the sending interface and its default network
  :                            288 . address
  :                            289 
  :                            290 . so in that case an error gets returned as described
  :                            291 . in the rest of this discussion
  :                            292 
  :                            293 . it's the interface number that is important in
  :                            294 . finding a local address for the datagram and
  :                            295 . for the checksum
  :                            296 
  :                            297 . it's also optimal if the interface index is already
  :                            298 . in the descriptor
  :                            299 
  :                            300 . we have the local address and the interface number
  :                            301 . already when we just bounce back reversing the
  :                            302 . incoming addresses as a server with the calls to
  :                            303 
  :                            304 . 	udp_rtxbuffer->udp_response_buffer
  :                            305 
  :                            306 . and after message construction, to here:
  :                            307 
  :                            308 .	udp_acw_send->udp_txaw
  :                            309 
  :                            310 . some implementations suggest you do a connect
  :                            311 . which makes a routing call to IP and plugs the
  :                            312 . default network address of the interface back
  :                            313 . into the socket
  :                            314 
  :                            315 . having a from address in the socket is
  :                            316 . usable when you are making the conventional
  :                            317 . calls send and sendto
  :                            318 
  :                            319 . the connect idea is nice for send but not
  :                            320 . for sendto which is meant for random desinations
  :                            321 . reached over various interfaces
  :                            322 
  :                            323 . it's also not right that an application should
  :                            324 . need to pick a local network address before
  :                            325 . binding a UDP socket. The application and its
  :                            326 . developer cannot know that unless the
  :                            327 . application is for one host with one interface
  :                            328 
  :                            329 . since this is the final path step of all the
  :                            330 . possible interface calls to send UDP
  :                            331 
  :                            332 . and the step where checksum is done if opted
  :                            333 . and the come-from address therefore needed
  :                            334 
  :                            335 . we'll do the following here
  :                            336 
  :                            337 . we'll return with an error code if there is
  :                            338 . no source address, because
  :                            339 
  :                            340 . if you got here with the primitive
  :                            341 
  :                            342 .	udp_acw_send
  :                            343 
  :                            344 . then it was up to you to copy the address
  :                            345 . information into the buffers. At this
  :                            346 . point it's no longer certain that the
  :                            347 . socket/port structure is based. That's
  :                            348 . not even in the parameters to this call
  :                            349 . and it's intended that the caller decides
  :                            350 . what at this point is based in socket
  :                            351 . space
  :                            352 
  :                            353 . so we'll send back an error
  :                            354 
  :                            355 . if you obtained the buffer with
  :                            356 
  :                            357 .	udp_rtxbuffer
  :                            358 
  :                            359 . then that DID set the addresses up for you
  :                            360 
  :                            361 . if you came from send, send had access
  :                            362 . to your socket and copied the addresses
  :                            363 . in there
  :                            364 
  :                            365 . if you then get to here with no come-from
  :                            366 . address you hadn't done the connect call
  :                            367 . which writes both to and from addresses
  :                            368 . to the socket. So the same error return
  :                            369 . is appropriate, we'll call it something
  :                            370 . like
  :                            371 
  :                            372 . UDP_NOSOURCENET	$equ	-5
  :                            373 
  :                            374 . if you got here from sendto, that makes
  :                            375 . the same internal routing call as connect
  :                            376 . for every datagram
  :                            377 . but writes what it finds to the datagram
  :                            378 . address buffer and not to the socket
  :                            379 
  :                            380 . recvfrom can cheekily write all the
  :                            381 . incoming addressing to the socket
  :                            382 
  :                            383 . then sendto can skip the internal routing
  :                            384 . step if the socket has a source address
  :                            385 . and the destinaton address stored on the
  :                            386 . socket is the sendto parameter address
  :                            387 
  :                            388 . send can just send on the recvfrom
  :                            389 . addresses as if it had connected
  :                            390 
  :                            391 . since the routing call finds the next hop
  :                            392 . as well as the interface, that information
  :                            393 . stays in the address buffer of the datagram
  :                            394 . and IP routing will not need to find the
  :                            395 . next hop again for this one datagram
  :                            396 
  :                            397 . for connect/send the next-hop isn't saved,
  :                            398 . because it might change. But the interface
  :                            399 . is saved
  :                            400 
  :                            401 
  :                            402 
  :                            403 udp_txaw

  :                            177 
  :                            178 	.	0:	is the remote ACK possible?
  :                            179 
  :                            180 	.	0:5	is there a remote reset?
  :                            181 
  :                            182 	.	1:	cache remote ACK and window
  :                            183 
  :                            184 	.	2:1	send waiting segments as remote
  :                            185 	.		window allows
  :                            186 
  :                            187 	.	2:2	release segments queued for ACK
  :                            188 	.		according to remote ACK value
  :                            189 	.		vs sequence + size of sent output
  :                            190 
  :                            191 	.	3:	compare remote sequence with local
  :                            192 	.		ACK
  :                            193 
  :                            194 	.	4:	if less, shave the leading edge
  :                            195 	.		of the input
  :                            196 
  :                            197 	.	5:	if more but sequence + length
  :                            198 	.		within local ACK + window, deferq
  :                            199 	.		and otherwise drop
  :                            200 
  :                            201 	.	6:	accept as much of the net input at 4:
  :                            202 	.		as local window allowed. This avoids
  :                            203 	.		accepting single bytes which are
  :                            204 	.		sent to push the window open
  :                            205 	.		and also avoids rejecting payload
  :                            206 	.		for which we have advertised
  :                            207 	.		window. Drop the segment if no
  :                            208 	.		part of it is accepted, and shave
  :                            209 	.		the trailing edge if part of it
  :                            210 	.		is rejected
  :                            211 
  :                            212 	.		It might be unwise to accept beyond
  :                            213 	.		window. A well behaved remote system
  :                            214 	.		won't send beyond window (apart from
  :                            215 	.		experimental 1-byters, and the response
  :                            216 	.		to those is drop them and advertise
  :                            217 	.		new window). A malicious remote system
  :                            218 	.		might try to manipulate window
  :                            219 
  :                            220 	.	7:	Advance local ACK and reduce local
  :                            221 	.		window according to the amount accepted
  :                            222 
  :                            223 	.	8:	generate new local window
  :                            224 
  :                            225 	.	9:	transmit segments queued for remote window
  :                            226 	.		according to remote ACK + window,  and
  :                            227 	.		if there is none, and no further input
  :                            228 	.		segment after this has yet been received
  :                            229 	.		for this connection, send an ACK, unless
  :                            230 	.		the incoming segment is an ACK without
  :                            231 	.		payload and its SEQ is not beyond local ACK
  :                            232 
  :                            233 	.		SYN and FIN have a payload weight of
  :                            234 	.		1 byte
  :                            235 
  :                            236 	.		restart connection:timer(1)
  :                            237 	.		maybe do this at an early point, depending
  :                            238 	.		whether the attempt to communicate
  :                            239 	.		counts as keepalive even if it is
  :                            240 	.		out of range
  :                            241 
  :                            242 
  :                            243 
  :                            244 
  :                            245 	.	1	is the remote ACK possible?
  :                            246

  :                            1753 
  :                            1754 
  :                            1755 .	buffer queues which are shared between tasks have to be accessed
  :                            1756 .	in a protected way. They are mainly the queues which both the IP
  :                            1757 .	task and an application protocol access. Some application protocols
  :                            1758 .	are in the IP task and some are not
  :                            1759 
  :                            1760 .	it would be quite bad to place TS cells in every queue structure
  :                            1761 .	because not all queues need them. Sockets and connections are the
  :                            1762 .	best available place for these lock cells
  :                            1763 
  :                            1764 .	all this code is SMP, so the code
  :                            1765 
  :                            1766 .	1.	prevents itself from getting interrupted or switched
  :                            1767 .	2.	locks the connection
  :                            1768 .	3.	updates the queue
  :                            1769 .	4.	frees the lock
  :                            1770 .	5.	reloads the interrupt mask it had before
  :                            1771 
  :                            1772 
  :                            1773 .	tcp_write_rxq is called from TCP in the IP task
  :                            1774 
  :                            1775 tcp_write_rxq
  :                            1776

  :                            46 
  :                            47 .	these blocking-only calls are central to the
  :                            48 .	known classical POSIX-like socket interface
  :                            49 
  :                            50 .	they correspond to the vectors	listen()
  :                            51 .					accept()
  :                            52 .					close()
  :                            53 .					connect()
  :                            54 
  :                            55 .	their blocking characteristic is in this file connect.msm
  :                            56 
  :                            57 .	and they complement		recv()
  :                            58 .	and				send()
  :                            59 
  :                            60 .	which block or not per flags argument
  :                            61 
  :                            62 .	first parameter of these routines is the handle integer
  :                            63 
  :                            64 .	the target connections of these routines are
  :                            65 .	in the default connections array
  :                            66 .	not the high-volume connection arrays
  :                            67 
  :                            68 
  :                            69

  :                            88 send1byte
00:00293E 150008              +89 	rsr	8
00:00293F 260008              +90 	sal	8
00:002940 20B000              +91 	sa	0, y
00:002941 C00005              +92 	aa	b
00:002942 350000              +93 	lret	0
  :                            94 
  :                            95 
  :                            96 .	for write to socket buffer
  :                            97 .	16 bits at a time are read from user data
  :                            98 .	via the staging register
  :                            99 
  :                            100 .	PSR designator b$streamZR is on
  :                            101 .	b$stream16r gets cleared here
  :                            102 .	this is for 24-bit opaque data
  :                            103 .	
  :                            104 
  :                            105 .	in TCP only
  :                            106 .	the caller may write an odd number of bytes
  :                            107 .	into the transmit segment buffer, so there
  :                            108 .	may be a data word half written from the 
  :                            109 .	previous send call
  :                            110 
  :                            111 .	the pointer is apdata[word] + bytes[byte]
  :                            112 .	descriptor:bytes is updated
  :                            113 
  :                            114 .	the segment may be filled and queued, and

  :                            495 XDR_TCP_INLINE $set 0
  :                            496 	$if	XDR_TCP_INLINE
  :                            497 
  :                            498 .	there is no detailed guidance from IETF, SUN or anywhere else
  :                            499 .	whether marked record fragments can split fields up
  :                            500 
  :                            501 .	Apple's NFS / TCP is written as if that was possible, so this
  :                            502 .	is also written as if a new record mark could start in the
  :                            503 .	middle of an opaque or number field
  :                            504 
  :                            505 .	these XDR calls will only work on a blocking connection
  :                            506 .	so the socket owner may not be the IP stack itself for these
  :                            507 
  :                            508 
  :                            509

  :                            574 	and, xi	3	. flag up not 4 multiple

  :                            576

  :                            580 			. we tested the marked record
  :                            581 			. for all present, so if no
  :                            582 			. segment buffer is at the
  :                            583 			. queue head, we are scanning
  :                            584 			. the wrong structure
  :                            585

  :                            723 .	that routine consumes the buffers in the user datagram
  :                            724 .	unconditionally and uses the cursor :readpoint

  :                            728 .	the buffers are not dropped, in case it's flagged

  :                            135 				. buffer size of

00:002BA7 0E0005              +140 	sbr	NET_LOG
  :                            141 
00:002BA8 CCA001              +142 	ab, i	pool:free(1)-1	. free chain for size
00:002BA9 64C008              +143 	la, i	ACW_SIZE, a	. next descriptor up
00:002BAA 400005              +144 	lr	b
  :                            145 
  :                            146 .	now keep looking to see if the new heap top
  :                            147 .	buffer is on its free chain. If it is, it
  :                            148 .	is the one at the far end, because the
  :                            149 .	highest-positioned buffers are the last
  :                            150 .	choice for re-use
  :                            151 
  :                            152 .	the stack of descriptors grows down towards
  :                            153 .	the heap of buffers which grows up
  :                            154 
  :                            155 .	the stack of descriptors packs back up
  :                            156 .	away from the heap of buffers which
  :                            157 .	packs down
  :                            158 
  :                            159 
  :                            160 
  :                            161 sell1_free?			. move on chain

  :                            168 				. the stack top
  :                            169

  :                            171 				. chain so stop now

  :                            178

00:002BE1 68C000              +249 	lb	0, a
00:002BE2 28B000              +250 	sb	queue:head
00:002BE3 350000              +251 readqz	lret	0
  :                            252 
  :                            253         $if      QSIMULTANEOUS
  :                            254 
  :                            255 .	these use the i_f index word as a next pointer
  :                            256 .	instead of the next pointer at the front of the
  :                            257 .	descriptor
  :                            258 
  :                            259 .	this is so that TCP segments awaiting ACK
  :                            260 .	can be on the interface queue and the
  :                            261 .	connection's ACK wait queue at the same time
  :                            262 
  :                            263 .	the bytes count in the descriptor has
  :                            264 .	bit 23 on to instruct the interface not
  :                            265 .	to drop the buffer after it is sent
  :                            266 
  :                            267 .	in the case of high volume banks where a
  :                            268 .	collection of TCP connections shares a
  :                            269 .	switched output bank at B80000 for segments
  :                            270 .	awaiting first window then ACK, separate
  :                            271 .	from the fixed transmit buffers at F00000
  :                            272 
  :                            273 .	where the two owners of the buffer are
  :                            274 .	the socket and the interface, it is the
  :                            275 .	interface which uses the next_bdi pointer
  :                            276 .	in the descriptor
  :                            277 
  :                            278 .	when actions are carried out on the socket
  :                            279 .	its transmit buffer bank at B80000 is always
  :                            280 .	based. This is only an issue of high volume
  :                            281 .	transaction connections which are intended to
  :                            282 .	exist in very large numbers and therefore
  :                            283 .	cannot keep all their transmitted segments
  :                            284 .	in the fixed  megaword at F00000 until remote
  :                            285 .	ACK arrives from the network
  :                            286

  :                            414 
  :                            415 .	here is a large difference between
  :                            416 .	first and subsequent store of any
  :                            417 .	managed object
  :                            418 
  :                            419 .	when you first store a managed object
  :                            420 .	you store its rules and the address
  :                            421 .	where its data is
  :                            422 
  :                            423 .	when you subsequently store the same
  :                            424 .	managed object, you write new data
  :                            425 .	at the address in the descriptor
  :                            426 .	and the rules initially stored apply
  :                            427
